title: Chinese Supertankers Run Houthi Gauntlet as Three Chokepoints Squeeze Global Oil, US-Saudi Nuclear Deal Signed
subhead: Two Chinese VLCCs carrying 4 million barrels of Saudi crude navigated the Bab el-Mandeb Strait as Houthi missile strikes hit a Saudi tanker, the Black Sea CPC terminal suspended operations after Ukrainian drone attacks on tankers, and the US formally signed a nuclear cooperation pact with Saudi Arabia that could allow uranium enrichment — all while Brent settles near $92 and the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively blocked.

sections.heading: Houthis Strike Saudi Tankers, Red Sea Passage Becomes Active Combat Zone
sections.body:
Yemen's Houthi movement claimed military operations targeting two Saudi-flagged oil tankers — the Encelia and the Layla — in the Red Sea on July 22-23, marking the first confirmed attacks on commercial vessels since the group declared a naval blockade against Saudi Arabia. A maritime security source told Reuters that the Encelia was struck by a missile, making it the first verified Houthi hit on a Saudi tanker in this conflict cycle.

The Houthis stated the attacks were in response to the vessels' 'violation' of their blockade of Saudi shipping. Four additional tankers reversed course in the Red Sea on July 22 to avoid the threat zone, according to Reuters. Meanwhile, two Chinese very large crude carriers — the VLCC Cosnew Lake and the Singapore-flagged Xin Long Yang — carrying a combined 4 million barrels of Saudi crude loaded at Yanbu are heading toward the Bab el-Mandeb Strait to attempt an exit, with the Xin Long Yang resuming its southbound voyage after briefly pausing and turning back on Tuesday, per LSEG shipping data reported by Reuters.

The calculus is stark: going south through Bab el-Mandeb runs the Houthi gauntlet, while going north through the Suez Canal adds significant time and cost for Asia-bound cargoes. Standard Chartered analysts now describe the situation as a 'two-chokepoint problem' — with both Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb simultaneously threatened.
sections.whyItMatters: The Houthi blockade has moved from threats to confirmed kinetic action against Saudi shipping. With Hormuz already at a trickle, losing the Red Sea bypass would trap the vast majority of Gulf crude exports. The Chinese VLCCs attempting the passage are a real-time test of whether commercial traffic can still transit Bab el-Mandeb at all.

sections.heading: Three Chokepoints Now Simultaneously Disrupted — Hormuz, Bab el-Mandeb, Black Sea
sections.body:
For the first time in the conflict, three major energy chokepoints are disrupted simultaneously. The Strait of Hormuz sees near-single-digit daily vessel transits, with Lloyd's of London estimating traffic is down 90% year-on-year, according to Foreign Policy. The Bab el-Mandeb Strait is under active Houthi blockade with missile strikes on tankers. And the Black Sea's CPC terminal at Novorossiysk has suspended operations after Ukrainian drone attacks struck four tankers in four days, halting the 1.7 million barrels per day of Kazakh crude that flows through it.

Reuters reported that Kazakhstan suspended oil pumping to the CPC marine terminal following the drone strikes. The Kazakh Foreign Ministry called the strikes 'an unacceptable infringement on the economic interests of the Republic of Kazakhstan' and did not rule out demanding compensation. Romania, which sources 63% of its oil imports from Kazakhstan via CPC, now faces what economist Adrian Negrescu called an 'unprecedented crisis' in its fuel market, per TASS.

Collectively, these three chokepoints imperil roughly a quarter of global oil supply, according to multiple analyst estimates cited by Bloomberg and Oilprice.com. Russia's Novorossiysk port has also imposed an informal ban on nighttime navigation due to the attacks.
sections.whyItMatters: The simultaneous disruption of three separate maritime corridors is unprecedented in modern energy markets. It means there is no longer a meaningful 'spare route' for redirecting Middle Eastern crude, and the Black Sea outage removes a key non-Middle East supply source at the worst possible time.

sections.heading: US-Saudi Nuclear Pact Signed — With Uranium Enrichment Provision
sections.body:
The Trump administration formally signed a 30-year civilian nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia on July 22, according to AP, the New York Times, and multiple other outlets. The deal, which now faces a 90-day congressional review period, could allow Saudi Arabia to pursue uranium enrichment for its civilian nuclear program, following a joint US-Saudi study.

Two AP sources confirmed the deal's approval. The agreement is expected to involve US firms in developing Saudi nuclear infrastructure. Notably, the pact is not expected to include the IAEA's Additional Protocol, which would allow for more intrusive monitoring and verification — a gap that nuclear proliferation experts say creates a destabilizing precedent, as reported by the New York Times.

The timing is deliberate: announced amid the 11th consecutive night of US strikes on Iran, the deal signals to Riyadh that Washington is willing to elevate its nuclear partnership even while denying enrichment to Tehran. Israel has expressed alarm, according to the Times of Israel. Nuclear experts warn it 'throws fuel on the smoldering fire of atomic proliferation,' as the NYT reported.
sections.whyItMatters: The US is creating a double standard on enrichment — denying it to Iran while greenlighting it for Saudi Arabia — that undermines the non-proliferation architecture. The absence of the IAEA Additional Protocol is a significant weakening of safeguards. And doing this while actively bombing Iran over its nuclear program sends a message that may accelerate, not deter, regional nuclear ambitions.

sections.heading: DP World Signs 50-Year Fujairah Terminal Deal as Gulf States Race to Bypass Hormuz
sections.body:
Dubai-based DP World announced a 50-year concession agreement with the Fujairah Ports Authority on July 22 to build two deepwater terminals on the UAE's eastern coast — outside the Strait of Hormuz. The Al Rugaylat container and multipurpose terminal and the Dibba general cargo terminal will have combined annual capacity of 2.5 million TEU containers, 190,000 vehicles, and 5.3 million tons of general cargo, according to DP World's statement.

The terminals will link to Jebel Ali, DP World's flagship port on the Persian Gulf coast, through an inland logistics network connected to the nearby Jafza zone. Phased development will take up to 30 months. Meanwhile, the UAE is already building a second pipeline to Fujairah that will double export capacity to 3.6 million bpd by 2027, and ADNOC is considering a third pipeline for refined products, according to Middle East Eye.

Saudi Arabia is eyeing a 2 million bpd increase in its East-West Pipeline capacity, currently running at 5 million bpd to Yanbu, Reuters reported. Kuwait's petroleum CEO Sheikh Nawaf al-Sabah told an Atlantic Council event in June that Kuwait is 'in discussions with our brothers in Saudi Arabia and in the Emirates to look at how to expand the pipeline system that they have to accommodate Kuwaiti barrels.' Iraq signed a deal with Syria in July to rehabilitate a pipeline from northern oil fields to the Mediterranean coast at Baniyas, as MEE first reported.
sections.whyItMatters: The bypass-Hormuz infrastructure race is now the biggest capital expenditure story in Gulf energy. DP World's 50-year bet on Fujairah signals that the region's logistics giants believe Hormuz disruptions are structural, not temporary. But as the Center for the National Interest's Greg Priddy notes, 'the caveat to all these bypasses is that they are still vulnerable to Iranian missiles and drones' — and Fujairah is well within Iranian missile range.

sections.heading: Oil Prices Hit Six-Week Highs as SPR Runs Low and Diesel Crisis Deepens
sections.body:
Brent crude settled above $92 per barrel on July 22, reaching a six-week high, with September WTI closing up $2.49 (2.95%) as global supply risks intensified across all three chokepoints. US gasoline prices returned to $4 per gallon, per AAA data, and diesel markets are under even greater strain.

The US Strategic Petroleum Reserve has been drawn down to dangerously low levels, with only about a month's worth of stocks remaining to compensate for missing Middle Eastern barrels, according to Foreign Policy's analysis. The Department of Energy reportedly redefined the SPR's minimum operating level downward — below what oil industry professionals consider prudent.

Refining margins have hit record highs as capacity tightens globally due to Western refinery closures, Ukraine's strikes on Russian refineries, China's reduction in refined product exports, and the effective closure of Hormuz limiting Middle Eastern refined product flows. Russia's temporary diesel export ban earlier in July compounded the shortage. Analysts at Saxo Bank noted that Brent is up over 50% year-on-year through July.

The Pentagon has spent $37.5 billion on the Iran war to date, Secretary Pete Hegseth told senators during a heated hearing, as the administration requests additional funding.
sections.whyItMatters: The market's shock absorbers are nearly gone. SPR reserves are at 40-year lows, refining capacity is squeezed worldwide, and three chokepoints are disrupted simultaneously. The structural price floor has shifted upward — even if a ceasefire materializes, diesel and fuel prices won't quickly revert given the infrastructure damage and reserve depletion.

sections.heading: Trump Orders End to Defense Critical Mineral Waivers from China, Russia
sections.body:
President Trump signed an executive order requiring the Department of Defense to stop issuing most waivers for critical minerals sourced from 'non-allied countries' — explicitly naming China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea — by January 1, 2027. The order targets samarium cobalt magnets, NdFeB magnets, tantalum, tungsten, and molybdenum, according to SMM Rare Earth Express.

Under the new rules, after 2027, only defense contractors who have submitted supply chain rectification plans approved by the Pentagon, documented material sources, and set a clear timetable for phasing out non-compliant materials may apply for waivers. The order also requires the Pentagon to develop a full-chain traceability system from raw materials to end-use products within 180 days.

Evolution Metals & Technologies Corp. announced it received the first non-China NdPr metal shipment for defense-compliant rare earth magnet production from SRE Vietnam, operationalizing an ex-China feedstock chain aligned with the new order. Meanwhile, Africa's rare earth projects are beginning to move beyond raw concentrate exports, with Namibia's Lofdal project planning to test production of higher-value rare earth carbonates.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most aggressive US move yet to decouple defense supply chains from Chinese rare earths. The January 2027 deadline is tight — the Pentagon's own assessments have previously flagged that fully replacing Chinese critical minerals could take a decade. The question is whether the order accelerates real supply chain diversification or simply creates compliance paperwork while defense contractors scramble for waivers.

sections.heading: Iran War Enters 11th Day as Trump Threatens Iranian Infrastructure Bombing
sections.body:
US Central Command launched fresh strikes against Iranian targets for an 11th consecutive day, stating the operations are 'designed to continue degrading Iran's ability to threaten commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.' President Trump vowed to bomb Iran's bridges and power plants if Tehran shoots at ships transiting the Strait, according to multiple outlets.

Yet the military campaign has not reopened Hormuz. Iran continues to fire missiles and drones at ships passing through the Strait, at the critical infrastructure of US-allied Gulf states, and at US military bases in the region. An Iranian strike severely damaged a desalination plant in Kuwait, a country 90% reliant on such facilities for drinking water, AP reported.

Former State Department official Alan Eyre, now at the Middle East Institute, characterized the strategy as fundamentally flawed: 'What are we trying to do? Escalate the pain on Iran to change its red lines. Tactically, we are trying to degrade their ability to interdict the Gulf.' But Iran has shown 'a surprising ability to continue firing' despite months of US strikes. Eyre described the core problem as a 'two-clocks issue' — whether Iran capitulates before the US runs out of strategic reserves. 'The two-clocks issue works in favor of Iran. We are running out of stocks,' he told Foreign Policy.
sections.whyItMatters: The 11-day bombing campaign has not achieved its stated objective of reopening Hormuz. Iran's asymmetric capability to threaten shipping persists despite massive US military action, and the cost — $37.5B and counting — is mounting with no clear exit. The gap between military escalation and strategic outcome keeps widening.

bottomLine: The energy landscape worsened materially on July 22-23 across three fronts simultaneously. Houthi missiles confirmed their first hit on a Saudi tanker, converting the Red Sea from a threat zone into an active combat zone — even as two Chinese supertankers attempted to run the Bab el-Mandeb gauntlet with 4 million barrels of crude. The Black Sea's CPC terminal, a 1.7 million bpd lifeline for Kazakh oil and Romanian fuel supplies, shut down after Ukrainian drone strikes on tankers. With Hormuz at 10% capacity, the global oil market has no spare routes left. Meanwhile, the US-Saudi nuclear pact — with its enrichment provision and missing IAEA safeguards — adds a proliferation dimension to an already combustible region. Oil prices at six-week highs and SPR reserves near their floor mean the economic runway is shrinking just as the military campaign shows no sign of achieving its stated objective of reopening shipping lanes.
sources: AP, Reuters, Foreign Policy, Oilprice.com, Middle East Eye, New York Times, Bloomberg, TASS, SMM Rare Earth Express, ING, Standard Chartered, Rystad Energy, Eurasia Group, DP World, LSEG, IER, TT News, DevDiscourse, CNBC, AAA, Lloyd's of London, Times of Israel, USA Today, NPR, Atlantic Council, Center for the National Interest
